addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

## claude.ai Figma
The official Figma MCP server. Use this server whenever the user wants to create, generate, edit, implement, or sync any design, UI, screen, component, mockup, or visual — in Figma, FigJam, Figma Make, or Figma Slides — and whenever the user mentions Figma or provides a figma.com URL.

This server bridges code and design in both directions, and supports designing from scratch using existing design systems and codebases.

CAPABILITIES:
- Read designs FROM Figma into code (get_design_context, get_screenshot, get_metadata, get_figjam)
- Write designs INTO Figma from code, intent, or existing components (use_figma, generate_figma_design, create_new_file, upload_assets)
- Bridge code and design via Code Connect (get_code_connect_map, add_code_connect_map)
- Create diagrams and FigJam content (generate_diagram, get_figjam)

WHEN TO USE THESE TOOLS:
- The user wants to create, mock up, or generate any UI, screen, component, or design — even if Figma isn't named
- The user wants to implement a design as code (design-to-code)
- The user wants to push a page, view, or component into Figma (code-to-design)
- The user wants to update, sync, or edit an existing Figma file
- The user shares a figma.com URL
- The user wants to build or extend a design system, design tokens, or component library
- The user wants to create a diagram in FigJam

SKILLS (Prefer the skills shipped with the Figma plugin on the agent being used. If no Figma plugin is installed, use the skills served by the Figma MCP resource):
- /figma-use — MANDATORY before calling use_figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-use/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-design — for translating an app page or layout into Figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-design/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-library — for building a design system in Figma from code; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-library/SKILL.md
- /figma-code-connect — for mapping Figma components to codebase components; fallback: skill://figma/figma-code-connect/SKILL.md
- /figma-use-figjam — for FigJam-specific use_f… [truncated]

## Net so far
**52 failed fetches (Cloudflare/ResearchGate challenge cards) were grounding report prose as citable T7 "sources"; they are now deleted and disclosed.** The faithfulness engine could never have caught this — strict_verify checks sentence↔span fidelity, and a sentence *can* be entailed by the text of a challenge card, so chrome passed the only hard gate and still grounded prose on nothing.

Regression: 75 failed / 2269 passed vs **77 / 2266 at pre-flywheel baseline (0e0eca9)** — zero new failures; the 75 are pre-existing and unrelated (audit_ir registry + import-path collection errors).

## Next
- Re-run outline-only (PG_STOP_AFTER_ROUTED_OUTLINE=1) to confirm the 52 rows are gone end-to-end, then Fable-gate the new outline for richness before any compose.
- Open quality gap (still unaddressed, from the laptop handoff): verified MATH/STAT synthesis (the outliner collects numbers but does not compute them), and report length ~4k words vs competitors' 10-22k.
- The 4 sciencedirect rows share the stale "Just a moment..." TITLE but have genuinely recovered bodies — 3 are correctly KEPT, but their **title is still chrome** and will render into the bibliography as "Just a moment...". Title repair (NOT deletion) is a separate small step.

- 2026-07-12 | Rank6 DIAGNOSIS — **REFUTED BY FABLE, AND MY EVIDENCE WAS A MEASUREMENT ARTIFACT** | I claimed the outline agent's gap-fill depth arm was dead: the checklist's three arms (coverage|density|numeric_rows) all pass through `_quote_is_grounded` (outline_agent.py:1454) BEFORE `kind` is parsed (:1458), so a DENSITY gap ("this section has too few sources") must quote the QUESTION verbatim — which a section's thinness never names. Cited "28 coverage / 1 numeric_rows / **0 density** across every log ever". **That count was garbage:** the accepted-gap disclosure (:1506-1509) logs only `section::aspect` and **never logs `kind`** — so the only kind-tagged lines in any log are the ones echoed inside `dropped N ungrounded line(s)`. I counted the REJECTS and reported them as the population. Fable caught it, and found the stronger fact underneath: **zero density lines appear even among the DROPPED lines — the model never emits them at all.** So the suppressor is the PROMPT (density is defined as "backed by only 1-2 sources" (:1322) while every worked example is `coverage`), not the code order — and my headline fix (reorder the parse) would have been a **no-op**.
- 2026-07-12 | Rank6 KILLED — **THE DEPTH CEILING IS COMPOSE, NOT RETRIEVAL** | Fable's decisive finding, verified by me against the source: word count is architecturally fixed and **evidence-independent**. `multi_section_generator.py:4` states the contract outright — *"produces 1500-3000-word reports"* — and the section writer's rule 8 (:3564, :3713) sets a **fixed** *"Target 10-18 sentences"* per section regardless of payload. 11 sections x 10-18 sentences x ~22 words = **2.4-4.4k words = exactly our observed ~4k.** Pushing MORE rows into sections (what Rank6 would have done) is the direction this project **already measured as WORSE**: WHEEL_PROGRESS.md:259-261 — *"THIN body (2141 words vs step3 3230) ... kept_fraction=0.35 -> 102 dropped sentences (step3=64), 77 verified (step3=91)"*. More rows drowned strict_verify and made the report SHORTER. **Rank6 was aimed at the wrong stage.**
- 2026-07-12 | Rank6a LATENT HOLE (found by Fable, verified by me) | My safety argument — "gap-fill rows are screened by the same armed topic judge at fold-in" — is **half-false in the half that matters**. `_fold_in.py:120` reads `topic_result.dropped_rows`, but `classify_topic_relevance` hard-codes `dropped_rows, dropped_titles = [], []` (topic_relevance_gate.py:709) unless `PG_SCOPE_TOPIC_GATE_HARD_DROP=1`, **which defaults to "0"** (:109). The chrome screen is real; **the off-topic screen at fold-in cannot delete anything.** Rank5's deletion is a *different* path that runs on the pre-built corpus BEFORE the outline loop, so live-retrieved rows never face it. Any future increase in retrieval MUST arm this first or it re-imports the exact alien rows Rank5 removed. Logged, not yet fixed — nothing currently depends on it (gap-fill fetched 0 rows this run).
- 2026-07-12 | THE MISSING MEASUREMENT (what Fable's ceiling argument does NOT settle) | Every compose measurement this project has ever taken — including the `kept_fraction=0.35` collapse that proves "more rows => thinner prose" — was taken on the **DIRTY corpus, the one that was ~50% alien**. The writer's menu held job listings, paralegal reading lists and Cloudflare captchas; it wrote sentences from them; strict_verify correctly killed them. **That is what kept_fraction=0.35 IS.** Rank5 removed precisely those rows — and **we have never once composed a report from the clean pool** (Rank5 stopped at outline-only). So the ceiling's own load-bearing number is confounded: on clean evidence the same fixed 10-18-sentence target should SURVIVE verification at a far higher rate, i.e. more verified words from the same prompt. Composing the clean pool is the cheapest experiment that can distinguish "compose is a hard architectural ceiling" from "compose was being fed garbage", and it must be run BEFORE any compose-stage change is designed. Launching it now.

Let me pin down the exact code semantics before I touch anything.

#     costliest false delete. A blanket high-tier exemption would use tier as a RELEVANCE proxy and
#     would shield precisely the credible-tier junk this audit found (the IMF climate WPs are T1/T2).
#     So tier is NOT a keep-veto; it is an ESCALATION criterion: a T1/T2 row the batch judge marks
#     OFF_SUBJECT is RE-judged alone on its full quote, and deletes only on a SECOND affirmative
#     verdict. Nobody is kept by tier and nobody is deleted by tier — the bar is just higher where a
#     mistake costs most.
# Default-OFF (``PG_PREBUILT_TOPIC_JUDGE``): unset => returns None => the generator passes ``()`` at
# the pool seam exactly as today => byte-identical.
_TOPIC_JUDGE_ENV = "PG_PREBUILT_TOPIC_JUDGE"
_TOPIC_DELETE_MAX_FRACTION_ENV = "PG_TOPIC_DELETE_MAX_FRACTION"
# Fixed so pass B is REPRODUCIBLE: the shuffle must be a different batch composition,
# not a different result run-to-run.
_TWOPASS_SEED = 1729


def _topic_judge_llm(model: str, max_tokens: int):
    """Synchronous ``str -> str`` LLM bridge for the judge (mirrors the live sweep's `_topic_llm`).

    The judge is a pure sync function by design (unit-testable with a stub), so the async
    OpenRouter client is driven on a worker thread with its own event loop."""
    import asyncio as _asyncio  # noqa: PLC0415
    import concurrent.futures as _futures  # noqa: PLC0415

    def _call(prompt: str) -> str:
        from src.polaris_graph.llm.openrouter_client import OpenRouterClient  # noqa: PLC0415

        async def _run() -> str:
            client = OpenRouterClient(model=model)
            try:
                resp = await client.generate(
                    prompt=prompt, max_tokens=max_tokens, temperature=0.0,
                )
                return (resp.content or "").strip()
            finally:
                if hasattr(client, "close"):
                    try:
                        await client.close()
                    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
                        pass

        with _futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1) as pool:
            return pool.submit(lambda: _asyncio.run(_run())).result()

    return _call

content.text: Careful — there are **two** different mechanisms here, and I nearly conflated them (the exact Rank6 trap). The `DEMOTED` line is the *scope gate*. `PG_PREBUILT_TOPIC_JUDGE` is a **separate** Rank4 semantic judge that returns an OFF_SUBJECT id set to the pool seam. Let me check whether that judge is actually running and deleting in the armed run.

> `[topic-judge] ARMED: judging 997 rows... only an affirmative OFF_SUBJECT deletes` → `PASS B: re-judging the 526 candidates in SHUFFLED batch composition (seed=1729). Deletable = affirmative OFF_SUBJECT in BOTH passes`

Ctrl has **zero** judge activity. So the A/B is real and correctly designed: **armed = clean-pool compose, ctrl = dirty-pool compose.** The `DEMOTED 610` line is just the scope gate's soft demotion running in *both* arms — a red herring, and the alien rows persist in the corpus *file* because the judge deletes in-memory at load, not on disk. Had I "fixed" this I'd have destroyed the one experiment that matters.

Hands off. Logging this and letting it run.

content.text:
Now the picture inverts, and it's important. Section 8 isn't a bucket of good-but-unrouted evidence — **it's where the alien junk self-sorts**: "gradeless learning" (T1!), "mass deworming" (T1), "Top Content Marketing Statistics – Forbes", "School Infrastructure on Learning", and multiple `Just a moment...` Cloudflare cards.

Two things follow, and both matter:

1. **The router is already quarantining the junk.** Off-topic rows can't match any real section, so they fall into the catch-all. Sections 1–7 are comparatively clean.
2. **Tier is not relevance.** 56 T1/T2 rows sit in the dump — high-credibility, totally off-topic (a Wiley journal, a deworming RCT). This confirms the codebase's own design note. A tier-based "quality firewall" would have *kept* every one of them.

The live question is whether section 8 gets *written* — because if it does, its junk sentences would die in `strict_verify` and that alone could be the `kept_fraction=0.35` collapse. That's testable right now from existing artifacts.

content.text:
This overturns my own hypothesis from ten minutes ago — and it's the most important measurement yet. A prior armed run's judge verdicts (507 deletable of 997) map onto the outline like this:

| section | rows | judged off-subject |
|---|---|---|
| 1 Introduction | 108 | **50.0%** |
| 2 Historical Patterns | 96 | **57.3%** |
| 3 Occupational Exposure | 97 | 13.4% |
| 5 Polarization/Wages | 64 | **50.0%** |
| 7 Conclusions | 29 | **55.2%** |
| 8 "Additional Findings" | 545 | 57.2% |

The router did **not** quarantine the junk — it spread it everywhere. **Half the evidence menu feeding every real section is off-subject.** The writer produces 10–18 sentences per section from a menu that's ~50% garbage, `strict_verify` correctly kills the junk-derived sentences, and that *is* `kept_fraction=0.35`. The confound hypothesis now has direct mechanical support.

Which makes one question load-bearing: does the armed run actually **exclude** those 507, or is the off-topic arm inert? If inert, both arms are identical and I'd burn an hour for nothing.

Both alive at 11.5 min (armed is LLM-bound in pass B; ctrl skipped the judge and is ahead into the compose loop). While they run, let me set up the comparator that will measure them the moment they land — and think about what the result can and cannot prove.

There are **two independent locks** on depth, and the A/B only tests one:

- **Lock A (quality):** dirty menu → `kept_fraction=0.35` → thin verified content. *That's what's under test now.*
- **Lock B (length):** the section writer's fixed `Target 10-18 sentences` rule. Even with a *perfect* pool and `kept_fraction=1.0`, 11 sections × 18 sentences × ~22 words ≈ **4.3k words maximum.**

So no amount of clean evidence can beat a 10–22k-word frontier report. Lock B has to be unlocked too, and cleaning the pool is what makes unlocking it *safe* — on a dirty menu, a higher sentence target just manufactures more junk that dies in verification.

    "  cover the topics the evidence supports and close the section with an\n"
    "  honest disclosure sentence like \"The mechanistic evidence available\n"
    "  for this synthesis is limited to [N] rows covering [TOPICS]; deeper\n"
    "  pharmacology detail would require additional primary sources.\""
)
# Stale back-reference cleanup: rule #8 no longer carries a "10-18 sentences"
# target in the concise variant, so the M-42c pointer to it is updated.
_CONCISE_MECHANISM_BACKREF = (
    "For other sections, the information-density guidance in rule #8 applies."
)


def _build_concise_variant(template: str) -> str:
    """I-ready-014 (#1083): derive the anti-verbosity / sharp-reporter variant of
    a section system-prompt template. Front-loads the decision (prepended to the
    CRITICAL RULES block) and REPLACES the length-maximizing language with
    information-density language. ASCII-only replacements; FAILS LOUD (raises) if
    any required length-bias anchor is absent, so a future template edit cannot
    silently no-op this transform (I-cap-005 lesson). Pure text transform — no env
    read, no faithfulness-gate touch."""
    out = template
    # (find_pattern, replacement, is_required) — re.subn so we can assert the
    # replacement actually fired exactly once on every REQUIRED anchor.
    operations: list[tuple[str, str, bool]] = [
        # Rule #8 length-bias sentence(s): "Target 10-18 ... match that depth ...
        # specific (quantitative )claims." -> information-density rule. Spans a
        # non-ASCII em-dash, so it is matched by regex rather than typed.
        (
            r"Target 10-18 sentences of source-anchored prose\..*?"
            r"(?:specific quantitative claims|specific claims)\.",
            _CONCISE_RULE_8,
            True,
        ),
        # Rule #10 (clinical only) length-bias tail: drop the em-dash + "top-tier
        # DR ... 50-200 citations ... not by writing more sentences." clause,
        # KEEP the multi-source-citation behavior before it; close on a period.
        (
            r"where evidence supports it.*?not by writing more sentences\.",
            _CONCISE_RULE_10_TAIL,
            False,
        ),
        # Mechanism depth rule (clinical only): drop ALL sentence-count bias — Codex iter-1 P1
        # (F13-P1-001) found the prior narrower match left the "target that matches pool size:"
        # preamble AND the trailing "...does not support 20-35 sentences." conditional in the ON prompt.
        # Span the WHOLE block from the preamble verb through that trailing sentence; the replacement
        # ("cover as many of the priority topics ... not sentence count") reads grammatically after
        # "...metabolism), ". Keeps the priority topics + thin-pool disclosure; drops every count.
        (
            r"use the depth\s+target that matches pool size:.*?"
            r"does not support 20-35 sentences\.",
            _CONCISE_MECHANISM_DEPTH,
            False,
        ),
        # Stale back-ref to rule #8's "10-18 sentences" target (Mechanism only).
        (
            r"For other sections, rule #8 target of 10-18 sentences applies "
            r"as usual\.",
            _CONCISE_MECHANISM_BACKREF,
            False,
        ),
    ]
    for pattern, replacement, required in operations:
        out, n = re.subn(pattern, replacement, out, flags=re.DOTALL)
        if required and n != 1:
            raise RuntimeError(
                "anti-verbosity transform anchor drifted: pattern "
                f"{pattern!r} replaced {n} times (expected exactly 1). The "
                "section template changed; update _build_concise_variant."
            )
    return _FRONT_LOADING_DIRECTIVE + out

This is the key discovery, and it's the **opposite** of what I expected: `_build_concise_variant` is an *anti-verbosity* transform — it strips rule #8's length target to make reports **shorter**. Meanwhile the base rule #8 reveals its own intent:

> *"Top-tier Deep Research reports (GPT-5.4 DR / Gemini 3.1 Pro DR) routinely reach this density — match that depth. Do NOT stop at 6-8 sentences"*

10–18 was written as a **floor-raiser** against the model's natural 6–8, but it functions as a **ceiling** because models anchor to the stated range. And the codebase already uses a **20–35** sentence target elsewhere (mechanism sections) — so higher targets are proven to work in this prompt system.

That completes the thesis. Before I build anything on it, I'm sending it to Fable to be attacked — I've been wrong twice today, both times caught by measurement rather than argument.

input.prompt:
You are the GATE for an autonomous research-system flywheel. Your job is to REFUTE, not to agree. Prior gates have caught this driver making confident claims from log greps that turned out to be measurement artifacts — twice today. Default to "refuted" if the evidence does not actually establish the claim. Be specific and cite file:line. Read the actual source files; do not trust my quotes.

REPO: /home/polaris/wt/flywheel (branch flywheel-v1). Read-only analysis; do NOT edit files, do NOT kill processes (two compose runs are in flight, PIDs 54370/54371).

BACKGROUND. The system writes deep-research reports (DeepResearch-Bench RACE, task 72: AI's impact on labor markets). Our reports are ~4000 words; frontier competitors (GPT/Gemini Deep Research) produce 10,000-22,000 words. We must beat them. A previous cycle ("Rank6") concluded the depth ceiling is in COMPOSE, not retrieval, citing:
 - src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py line 4: docstring "produces 1500-3000-word reports"
 - same file :3564 and :3713, section-writer rule #8: "Target 10-18 sentences of source-anchored prose"
 - and a prior measurement: pushing MORE evidence rows into sections made the report SHORTER (kept_fraction=0.35, 102 dropped sentences vs 64) — i.e. "more rows => thinner prose".

MY CURRENT THESIS (attack it). There are TWO INDEPENDENT LOCKS on depth:

LOCK A (quality/confound): every compose measurement ever taken by this project — INCLUDING the kept_fraction=0.35 collapse that "proves" more-rows-is-worse — was taken on a corpus that is ~51% off-topic. Evidence: outputs/agentic_report_20260712_203630/topic_judge_dispositions.json reports n_in=997, n_off_subject_deletable=507 (51%). I cross-referenced those 507 ev_ids against outputs/baseline_routed_outline.json (8 sections, 1032 routed rows) and the off-subject rows are spread through EVERY section, not quarantined: sec1 Introduction 50.0% off-subject, sec2 57.3%, sec3 13.4%, sec4 25.3%, sec5 50.0%, sec6 35.7%, sec7 55.2%, sec8 57.2%. Claim: the writer composes its fixed 10-18 sentences from a menu that is ~half alien, strict_verify correctly kills the alien-derived sentences, and THAT is what kept_fraction=0.35 IS — so the "more rows => thinner" finding is confounded and does not establish an architectural ceiling.

LOCK B (length): the 10-18-sentence target is FIXED and EVIDENCE-INDEPENDENT, so even a perfect clean pool with kept_fraction=1.0 caps the report at roughly 11 sections x 18 sentences x ~22 words ~= 4.3k words. Therefore cleaning the pool ALONE can NEVER reach 10-22k words, and the ONLY path to frontier length is to raise the sentence target — which is safe ONLY on a clean pool (on a dirty pool a higher target just manufactures more junk that dies in verify).

SUPPORTING OBSERVATION: multi_section_generator.py:3788 `_build_concise_variant` is an ANTI-verbosity transform that STRIPS rule #8's length target (makes reports shorter). And a "mechanism depth" rule elsewhere in the same file already uses a "20-35 sentences" target, which suggests higher targets are already viable in this prompt system.

ANSWER THESE, each with file:line evidence:
1. Is LOCK A's confound argument sound? Specifically: does strict_verify / the faithfulness gate actually DROP sentences derived from off-topic-but-real evidence? An off-topic source's text can still ENTAIL a sentence written from it (the sentence would be faithful but irrelevant). If strict_verify only checks sentence<->span entailment, then alien rows would produce sentences that PASS verification, and kept_fraction=0.35 would NOT be explained by alien evidence. This is the crux — trace the actual verification code and tell me whether off-topic evidence produces DROPPED sentences or KEPT-but-irrelevant sentences. If the latter, LOCK A is REFUTED and the clean-pool A/B now running will show NO kept_fraction improvement.
2. Is the ~4.3k arithmetic for LOCK B right? Find where the section count actually comes from at runtime, and whether any other mechanism (expansion passes, PG_SYNTHESIS_MAX_EXPANSION_PASSES=3, PG_TARGET_TOTAL_WORDS=8000, PG_MAX_WORDS_PER_SECTION=3000, continuation calls, PG_SKIP_EXPANSION_WORD_THRESHOLD=8000) can already grow a report past that cap. Note PG_TARGET_TOTAL_WORDS is set to 8000 yet reports come out ~4000 — WHY does that target not bind? That gap is the real question; find what actually enforces length.
3. Is raising rule #8's target the right lever, or is there a HARDER binding constraint downstream (max_tokens on the section writer call, PG_SECTION_WRITER_MAX_TOKENS=24576, PG_SECTION_TOKEN_BUDGET=6000, PG_MAX_WORDS_PER_SECTION, a truncation, or a post-hoc sanitizer/dedup that deletes sentences) that would make a raised target a NO-OP? A no-op fix is the exact failure mode that killed Rank6 — hunt for it specifically.
4. What is the single highest-value NEXT EXPERIMENT? Be concrete (file, line, env var, expected measurable delta).

Return a verdict: SOUND / PARTLY SOUND / REFUTED for each of LOCK A and LOCK B, with the reasoning that decides it.

Fable is attacking the thesis. The crux I handed it: *does off-topic evidence produce **dropped** sentences, or **kept-but-irrelevant** ones?* If alien sources still entail the sentences written from them, they'd **pass** verification — and Lock A collapses.

content.text:
Two hard facts, and they reframe everything:

1. The task-72 report is **2,446 words** — even thinner than the ~4k claimed. Its sections are tiny: 49w, 56w, 101w, 163w. At ~22 words/sentence those are **~2–15 sentences**, so the writer is **undershooting the existing 10–18 target**, not being capped by it.
2. **Zero alien sources survive into the report.** No gradeless-learning, no deworming, no paralegals.

That second fact splits Lock A cleanly. Either the writer *wrote* from alien rows and `strict_verify` killed those sentences (→ clean pool raises kept_fraction and sections grow) — or the writer *never wrote from them*, in which case cleaning the pool changes nothing about compose. The undershoot is the tell: if the writer emits ~26 sentences and verification keeps ~9, that's exactly `kept_fraction≈0.35`, and the clean pool would grow sections **without touching any prompt**.

Let me get the written-vs-kept sentence counts for that run.

content.text:
Monitor armed. Current state while both wait:

**Blocked:** GitHub push — the box has no PAT, no SSH key, no `gh`. I made a local bundle instead (`/home/polaris/backups/flywheel-v1-b1f3f68.bundle`, 183MB). *Operator: drop a PAT in and I'll push immediately.*

**In flight:** the clean-pool A/B (armed 490-row pool vs ctrl 997-row pool), plus a Fable gate attacking my thesis.

**What I found in the grounding read** — this is the substantive result so far, and it redirects the plan the operator handed me:

The proposed experiment #1 (source-eligibility firewall on tier / peer-reviewed / English) **would not have worked, and I'd recommend against building it.** The evidence says relevance, not credibility, is what's broken: 56 of the junk rows are **T1/T2 — the highest credibility tiers** — and they're things like a gradeless-learning RCT, a mass-deworming study, and a Wiley governance journal. A tier-based firewall keeps every one of them. The codebase's own design note already says this outright: *"tier is NOT a keep-veto; a blanket high-tier exemption would shield precisely the credible-tier junk this audit found."* The instrument that *does* work on relevance — the two-pass semantic judge — is already built and is what's running now.

The deeper finding: **51% of the evidence menu is off-subject, and it's spread through every section** (Introduction 50%, Historical Patterns 57%, Conclusions 55%), not quarantined in the catch-all. The task-72 report is only **2,446 words** with sections of 49–350 words — meaning the writer is *undershooting* its own 10–18-sentence target, not being capped by it. If it writes ~26 sentences and verification keeps ~9, that is precisely `kept_fraction=0.35`, and cleaning the pool would grow the report **without changing a single prompt**. That's the hypothesis the A/B settles.

I'll report the moment the numbers land.

---

# VERDICT

**LOCK A (off-topic corpus confounds kept_fraction=0.35): REFUTED** — the mechanism does not exist in the code.
**LOCK B (a fixed sentence target is the only live length governor): PARTLY SOUND** — right conclusion, wrong arithmetic, and you missed the *actual* reason PG_TARGET_TOTAL_WORDS doesn't bind.

---

## 1. LOCK A — REFUTED. strict_verify is topicality-blind.

You asked the right question and the answer kills your thesis. I traced the entire gate.

`strict_verify` (`/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/src/polaris_graph/generator/provenance_generator.py:3598`) takes exactly two inputs: `draft_text` and `evidence_pool`. **The research question is not a parameter.** It never reaches any verdict.

Per-sentence, `verify_sentence_provenance` (`provenance_generator.py:2098`) applies exactly these checks, all of them **sentence ↔ its own cited span**:

| check | line | compares against |
|---|---|---|
| ev_id in pool | :2211 | pool |
| span bounds | :2216 | that row's `direct_quote` |
| decimals/integers/percents in span | :2265–2371 | cited span |
| binding qualifier retained | :2415 | cited span |
| content-word overlap ≥ MIN | :2440–2460 | cited span |
| trial-name match | :2510 | cited row |
| **NLI entailment** | **:2586** | **`_get_judge().judge(sentence_clean, combined_span)`** |

The judge signature is `def judge(self, sentence: str, span: str)` (`src/polaris_graph/llm/entailment_judge.py:740`) and its prompt (`entailment_judge.py:584`) reads verbatim: *"You will be given a SPAN of source text and a SENTENCE that cites that span. Decide whether the SPAN entails the SENTENCE."* No question, no topic, no section title.

**Therefore: a sentence faithfully written from a real-but-alien source is ENTAILED by its span and is KEPT.** Off-topic evidence produces exactly the failure you named as the refuting case — *kept-but-irrelevant sentences, not dropped ones*. It cannot be what kept_fraction=0.35 is.

Nor do the downstream filters rescue LOCK A:
- **M-41c** (`multi_section_generator.py:5010`, `filter_underframed_trial_sentences`) drops only sentences that name a *clinical trial by short name* with <3 frame elements. Topicality-blind.
- `_screen_uncited_numeric_sentences` (`:6684`) drops uncited decimals. `_screen_render_chrome_prose` (`:6694`) drops web chrome. Neither reads the question.
- The one screen that *does* read the question — `_withhold_offtopic_spans` (`weighted_enrichment.py:880`, threaded at `multi_section_generator.py:6046`) — is **only reachable in the `_evsr` / verified-compose branches**, and both are OFF on your path (`_render_verified_spans_enabled` defaults OFF, `weighted_enrichment.py:1667`; `_verified_compose_enabled` defaults OFF, `verified_compose.py:217`; neither flag is in `.env` nor set by `scripts/compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py`). Your live writer is the legacy `_call_section` LLM path (`multi_section_generator.py:6294`).

**What kept_fraction=0.35 actually was.** Read the comment that reports it — `multi_section_generator.py:513–527`. It states the measurement condition: `PG_ROUTE_ALL_BASKETS` + `PG_EV_BUDGET_TRACKS_PAYLOAD` ON → section `ev_ids` ballooned to **52–103 rows** (vs ~15 in the healthy step3 run) → the writer was prompted over a crammed menu → 65% of what it wrote failed span-binding. That is a **menu-size** artifact, not a topicality artifact. And your compose script sets *both of those flags* (`scripts/compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py:530–531`).

**The kicker:** the fix for that exact drag already exists and has never been turned on. `PG_WRITER_TOPN_EV_PER_SECTION` (`multi_section_generator.py:541–546`, default 0 = OFF) caps the *writer's prompt menu* to the top-N ranked rows while leaving `evidence_pool` and the bibliography untouched. It was written specifically for the 0.35 collapse. Your compose script does not set it.

**Falsifiable prediction for the A/B now running (PIDs 54370/54371):** the armed (clean-pool) arm will show **no material kept_fraction improvement** (I'll say ±0.05). If it jumps to ~0.6+, I am wrong and LOCK A is live. Note the armed arm *does* delete the 507 rows from the pool before outline+compose (`multi_section_generator.py:10118`, `junk_deletion_gate.partition_rows`), so it is a real test — it just tests the wrong hypothesis. Its genuine payoff is *relevance of shipped prose*, which is a RACE-score win, not a length win.

---

## 2. LOCK B — the arithmetic is wrong; the conclusion survives; and you were hunting in the wrong pipeline.

### Why PG_TARGET_TOTAL_WORDS=8000 doesn't bind: nothing on your code path reads it.

This is the answer to your real question, and it isn't subtle. Every length governor you listed lives in a **different pipeline that your run never enters**:

| knob | defined | consumed by |
|---|---|---|
| `PG_TARGET_TOTAL_WORDS` | `state.py:48` | `agents/synthesizer.py:3543`, `synthesis/section_writer.py:666` |
| `PG_SYNTHESIS_MAX_EXPANSION_PASSES` | `state.py:152` | `agents/synthesizer.py:3548` |
| `PG_SKIP_EXPANSION_WORD_THRESHOLD` | — | `agents/synthesizer.py:3546` |
| `PG_MAX_WORDS_PER_SECTION` | `state.py:44` | `synthesis/section_writer.py:1595` |
| `PG_SECTION_TOKEN_BUDGET` | `state.py:273` | `synthesis/token_budget.py:27` → `section_writer.py:1305` |
| `PG_SECTION_WRITER_MAX_TOKENS` | `state.py:102` | `synthesis/section_writer.py:1570` |

`agents/synthesizer.py` and `synthesis/section_writer.py` are reached only via `graph.py:1995 → synthesize_report`. **`multi_section_generator.py` imports neither** (grep: zero hits), and `compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py:594` calls `generate_multi_section_report` directly. `multi_section_generator.py:11646` merely *sums* `total_words` as telemetry — it is a readout, never a target. **The expansion machinery, the 8000-word target, the per-section word cap, and the L0/L1/L2 token-budget tiering are all dark code on the flywheel path.** There is no word governor at all. Length is purely emergent.

### The real length equation

`body_words ≈ n_sections × sentences_per_section × words_per_sentence`, minus verify/dedup losses. Measured on the actual 11-section artifact `docs/step3_insight_lever_ab/control_report.md` (3875 words):

```
Introduction and Scope                 12 rendered sents   452 w   38 w/s
Task-Based Frameworks                  18 rendered sents   461 w   26 w/s   <-- AT the rule-#8 ceiling
Occupational Exposure                  11 rendered sents   336 w   31 w/s
Empirical Evidence on Employment       15 rendered sents   441 w   29 w/s
Productivity Effects of GenAI          13 rendered sents   470 w   36 w/s
Wage Inequality / Polarization         16 rendered sents   470 w   29 w/s   <-- at the ceiling
Policy Implications                     5 rendered sents   157 w
Cross-Study Synthesis                   3 rendered sents   113 w
Conclusions and Research Gaps           8 rendered sents   247 w
```

Two corrections to your LOCK B:
- **words/sentence is ~30, not 22.** Your 4.3k figure is ~35% low.
- These are **post-verify, post-dedup survivors**, and the evidence-rich sections **already land at 16–18 — the top of rule #8's band.** That is the single strongest fact in your favor: the target is *binding where the evidence is rich*. Corrected ceiling under current structure: 9 × 18 × 30 ≈ **4.9k body words**. Observed 3.1k body. Frontier is 10–22k. Cleaning the pool cannot close that. **Your conclusion stands; your numbers don't.**

Also correct: `_build_concise_variant` (`multi_section_generator.py:3788`) strips rule #8's target, and it fires only under `PG_ANTI_VERBOSITY` — which is **not set anywhere in this repo**. So rule #8 is live, not stripped. And the section count is **not** the constraint you feared: the facet outline's bound is `_facet_outline_max_sections()` = **40** (`:964`, applied `:1931-1933`), not 6. `M-41a`'s 5–6-section cap (`:1912`) is on the *legacy LLM outline*, not the facet outline you run.

---

## 3. Is raising rule #8 a no-op? No — but it is CONDITIONAL, and the condition is not what you think.

I hunted specifically for the Rank6 failure mode. Cleared:
- **max_tokens is not binding.** Your script passes `section_max_tokens=2400` (`compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py:601`), but `multi_section_generator.py:4369–4375` floors it: `content = max(2400, 16384 + 8192) = 24576`, with reasoning capped at 16384 → ~8k content tokens ≈ **6,000 words/section available**. An 18-sentence section uses ~550. Massive headroom.
- `PG_MAX_WORDS_PER_SECTION`, `PG_SECTION_TOKEN_BUDGET`, `PG_SECTION_WRITER_MAX_TOKENS` — all dark on this path (above).
- No hard per-section sentence cap exists anywhere in the render path.

**Two live post-hoc deleters *do* gate the lever:**

1. **B11 same-span dedup** — `multi_section_generator.py:6635` → `verified_compose.py:155 dedup_same_span_sentences`, **default ON** (`PG_COMPOSE_SAME_SPAN_DEDUP`). It keeps **exactly one sentence per distinct `(ev_id, start, end)` footprint**, unless a later same-footprint sentence introduces a *new number token* (`verified_compose.py:202–208`). This is the constraint that decides your experiment: **a raised target converts to words only if the extra sentences cite NEW SPANS.** Extra rewords of already-cited spans are deleted, silently, with a log line.
2. **fact_dedup / `PG_ANTI_RESTATEMENT`**, default ON (`multi_section_generator.py:3878–3886`, pass at `:11024–11060`): cross-section restatements are consolidated, and its safe-fail path *drops* the redundant sentence and keeps the primary.

So the honest framing is: **the lever is "cite more DISTINCT sources," not "write more sentences."** The headroom is real — sections carry **52–103 routed rows** and render only 11–18 sentences, so distinct un-rendered spans are abundant. But note rule #9 (`:3565`) currently asks for a floor of only **5 distinct sources per section**. Raising #8 without raising #9 invites the model to pad from the same spans, and B11 will eat it. That is precisely the Rank6 no-op shape, and it is avoidable.

---

## 4. Highest-value next experiment

**Not** the clean-pool A/B (already running; it answers a question whose mechanism I just refuted).

**Run a single-variable prompt A/B on the SAME corpus and SAME outline, changing only the section-writer length + diversity targets.**

- **Files/lines:** `multi_section_generator.py:3564` (clinical template rule #8) **and `:3713`** (field-agnostic template rule #8 — *both*, since selection is domain-dependent; editing one is a silent 50% no-op). Plus rule #9 at `:3565` / `:3714`.
- **Change:** `"Target 10-18 sentences"` → `"Target 28-36 sentences"`; rule #9 `"at least 5 DISTINCT sources"` → `"at least 15 DISTINCT sources"`. Precedent that the prompt system tolerates it: the M-47 Mechanism rule already ships a **20-35 sentence** target in the same template.
- **Prefer an env var** (`PG_SECTION_SENTENCE_TARGET`) over a hardcoded edit so the arms are one process apart and the winner is revertible.
- **Also set `PG_WRITER_TOPN_EV_PER_SECTION=20`** in a third arm — it is the purpose-built, never-activated fix for the 52–103-row crammed menu that *actually* caused kept_fraction=0.35.

**Instrument (all already logged):** per-section `sentences_verified` / `sentences_dropped`, `post-M-41c kept_fraction=` (`:6465`), `B11 same-span dedup: collapsed N` (`:6643`), M-41c drops (`:6617`), distinct `ev_ids` cited, final body words.

**Expected measurable delta:** body words **3.1k → 5.5–6.5k** (9 sections × ~24 kept × ~30 w). 
**The refutation condition — watch for this:** if body words stay flat while **`B11 collapsed` explodes**, then the ceiling is *distinct-span diversity*, not the prompt target, and the correct lever moves to the outline (more sections; the bound is 40, you use 9) and to per-section span coverage. That single counter is what makes this experiment non-fakeable.

**Bottom line:** the depth ceiling is in compose (Rank6 was right about the *location*), but it is neither the corpus (LOCK A: refuted, strict_verify cannot see topic) nor a mysterious governor (there is none — the 8000-word target is dead code on this path). It is **rule #8 × section count, bounded by distinct-span diversity via B11.**</result>

- The one screen that *does* read the question — `_withhold_offtopic_spans` (`weighted_enrichment.py:880`, threaded at `multi_section_generator.py:6046`) — is **only reachable in the `_evsr` / verified-compose branches**, and both are OFF on your path (`_render_verified_spans_enabled` defaults OFF, `weighted_enrichment.py:1667`; `_verified_compose_enabled` defaults OFF, `verified_compose.py:217`; neither flag is in `.env` nor set by `scripts/compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py`). Your live writer is the legacy `_call_section` LLM path (`multi_section_generator.py:6294`).

**What kept_fraction=0.35 actually was.** Read the comment that reports it — `multi_section_generator.py:513–527`. It states the measurement condition: `PG_ROUTE_ALL_BASKETS` + `PG_EV_BUDGET_TRACKS_PAYLOAD` ON → section `ev_ids` ballooned to **52–103 rows** (vs ~15 in the healthy step3 run) → the writer was prompted over a crammed menu → 65% of what it wrote failed span-binding. That is a **menu-size** artifact, not a topicality artifact. And your compose script sets *both of those flags* (`scripts/compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py:530–531`).

**The kicker:** the fix for that exact drag already exists and has never been turned on. `PG_WRITER_TOPN_EV_PER_SECTION` (`multi_section_generator.py:541–546`, default 0 = OFF) caps the *writer's prompt menu* to the top-N ranked rows while leaving `evidence_pool` and the bibliography untouched. It was written specifically for the 0.35 collapse. Your compose script does not set it.

**Falsifiable prediction for the A/B now running (PIDs 54370/54371):** the armed (clean-pool) arm will show **no material kept_fraction improvement** (I'll say ±0.05). If it jumps to ~0.6+, I am wrong and LOCK A is live. Note the armed arm *does* delete the 507 rows from the pool before outline+compose (`multi_section_generator.py:10118`, `junk_deletion_gate.partition_rows`), so it is a real test — it just tests the wrong hypothesis. Its genuine payoff is *relevance of shipped prose*, which is a RACE-score win, not a length win.

---

## 2. LOCK B — the arithmetic is wrong; the conclusion survives; and you were hunting in the wrong pipeline.

### Why PG_TARGET_TOTAL_WORDS=8000 doesn't bind: nothing on your code path reads it.

This is the answer to your real question, and it isn't subtle. Every length governor you listed lives in a **different pipeline that your run never enters**:

| knob | defined | consumed by |
|---|---|---|
| `PG_TARGET_TOTAL_WORDS` | `state.py:48` | `agents/synthesizer.py:3543`, `synthesis/section_writer.py:666` |
| `PG_SYNTHESIS_MAX_EXPANSION_PASSES` | `state.py:152` | `agents/synthesizer.py:3548` |
| `PG_SKIP_EXPANSION_WORD_THRESHOLD` | — | `agents/synthesizer.py:3546` |
| `PG_MAX_WORDS_PER_SECTION` | `state.py:44` | `synthesis/section_writer.py:1595` |
| `PG_SECTION_TOKEN_BUDGET` | `state.py:273` | `synthesis/token_budget.py:27` → `section_writer.py:1305` |
| `PG_SECTION_WRITER_MAX_TOKENS` | `state.py:102` | `synthesis/section_writer.py:1570` |

`agents/synthesizer.py` and `synthesis/section_writer.py` are reached only via `graph.py:1995 → synthesize_report`. **`multi_section_generator.py` imports neither** (grep: zero hits), and `compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py:594` calls `generate_multi_section_report` directly. `multi_section_generator.py:11646` merely *sums* `total_words` as telemetry — it is a readout, never a target. **The expansion machinery, the 8000-word target, the per-section word cap, and the L0/L1/L2 token-budget tiering are all dark code on the flywheel path.** There is no word governor at all. Length is purely emergent.

### The real length equation

`body_words ≈ n_sections × sentences_per_section × words_per_sentence`, minus verify/dedup losses. Measured on the actual 11-section artifact `docs/step3_insight_lever_ab/control_report.md` (3875 words):

```
Introduction and Scope                 12 rendered sents   452 w   38 w/s
Task-Based Frameworks                  18 rendered sents   461 w   26 w/s   <-- AT the rule-#8 ceiling
Occupational Exposure                  11 rendered sents   336 w   31 w/s
Empirical Evidence on Employment       15 rendered sents   441 w   29 w/s
Productivity Effects of GenAI          13 rendered sents   470 w   36 w/s
Wage Inequality / Polarization         16 rendered sents   470 w   29 w/s   <-- at the ceiling
Policy Implications                     5 rendered sents   157 w
Cross-Study Synthesis                   3 rendered sents   113 w
Conclusions and Research Gaps           8 rendered sents   247 w
```

Two corrections to your LOCK B:
- **words/sentence is ~30, not 22.** Your 4.3k figure is ~35% low.
- These are **post-verify, post-dedup survivors**, and the evidence-rich sections **already land at 16–18 — the top of rule #8's band.** That is the single strongest fact in your favor: the target is *binding where the evidence is rich*. Corrected ceiling under current structure: 9 × 18 × 30 ≈ **4.9k body words**. Observed 3.1k body. Frontier is 10–22k. Cleaning the pool cannot close that. **Your conclusion stands; your numbers don't.**

Also correct: `_build_concise_variant` (`multi_section_generator.py:3788`) strips rule #8's target, and it fires only under `PG_ANTI_VERBOSITY` — which is **not set anywhere in this repo**. So rule #8 is live, not stripped. And the section count is **not** the constraint you feared: the facet outline's bound is `_facet_outline_max_sections()` = **40** (`:964`, applied `:1931-1933`), not 6. `M-41a`'s 5–6-section cap (`:1912`) is on the *legacy LLM outline*, not the facet outline you run.

---

## 3. Is raising rule #8 a no-op? No — but it is CONDITIONAL, and the condition is not what you think.

I hunted specifically for the Rank6 failure mode. Cleared:
- **max_tokens is not binding.** Your script passes `section_max_tokens=2400` (`compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py:601`), but `multi_section_generator.py:4369–4375` floors it: `content = max(2400, 16384 + 8192) = 24576`, with reasoning capped at 16384 → ~8k content tokens ≈ **6,000 words/section available**. An 18-sentence section uses ~550. Massive headroom.
- `PG_MAX_WORDS_PER_SECTION`, `PG_SECTION_TOKEN_BUDGET`, `PG_SECTION_WRITER_MAX_TOKENS` — all dark on this path (above).
- No hard per-section sentence cap exists anywhere in the render path.

**Two live post-hoc deleters *do* gate the lever:**

1. **B11 same-span dedup** — `multi_section_generator.py:6635` → `verified_compose.py:155 dedup_same_span_sentences`, **default ON** (`PG_COMPOSE_SAME_SPAN_DEDUP`). It keeps **exactly one sentence per distinct `(ev_id, start, end)` footprint**, unless a later same-footprint sentence introduces a *new number token* (`verified_compose.py:202–208`). This is the constraint that decides your experiment: **a raised target converts to words only if the extra sentences cite NEW SPANS.** Extra rewords of already-cited spans are deleted, silently, with a log line.
2. **fact_dedup / `PG_ANTI_RESTATEMENT`**, default ON (`multi_section_generator.py:3878–3886`, pass at `:11024–11060`): cross-section restatements are consolidated, and its safe-fail path *drops* the redundant sentence and keeps the primary.

So the honest framing is: **the lever is "cite more DISTINCT sources," not "write more sentences."** The headroom is real — sections carry **52–103 routed rows** and render only 11–18 sentences, so distinct un-rendered spans are abundant. But note rule #9 (`:3565`) currently asks for a floor of only **5 distinct sources per section**. Raising #8 without raising #9 invites the model to pad from the same spans, and B11 will eat it. That is precisely the Rank6 no-op shape, and it is avoidable.

---

## 4. Highest-value next experiment

**Not** the clean-pool A/B (already running; it answers a question whose mechanism I just refuted).

**Run a single-variable prompt A/B on the SAME corpus and SAME outline, changing only the section-writer length + diversity targets.**

- **Files/lines:** `multi_section_generator.py:3564` (clinical template rule #8) **and `:3713`** (field-agnostic template rule #8 — *both*, since selection is domain-dependent; editing one is a silent 50% no-op). Plus rule #9 at `:3565` / `:3714`.
- **Change:** `"Target 10-18 sentences"` → `"Target 28-36 sentences"`; rule #9 `"at least 5 DISTINCT sources"` → `"at least 15 DISTINCT sources"`. Precedent that the prompt system tolerates it: the M-47 Mechanism rule already ships a **20-35 sentence** target in the same template.
- **Prefer an env var** (`PG_SECTION_SENTENCE_TARGET`) over a hardcoded edit so the arms are one process apart and the winner is revertible.
- **Also set `PG_WRITER_TOPN_EV_PER_SECTION=20`** in a third arm — it is the purpose-built, never-activated fix for the 52–103-row crammed menu that *actually* caused kept_fraction=0.35.

**Instrument (all already logged):** per-section `sentences_verified` / `sentences_dropped`, `post-M-41c kept_fraction=` (`:6465`), `B11 same-span dedup: collapsed N` (`:6643`), M-41c drops (`:6617`), distinct `ev_ids` cited, final body words.

**Expected measurable delta:** body words **3.1k → 5.5–6.5k** (9 sections × ~24 kept × ~30 w). 
**The refutation condition — watch for this:** if body words stay flat while **`B11 collapsed` explodes**, then the ceiling is *distinct-span diversity*, not the prompt target, and the correct lever moves to the outline (more sections; the bound is 40, you use 9) and to per-section span coverage. That single counter is what makes this experiment non-fakeable.

**Bottom line:** the depth ceiling is in compose (Rank6 was right about the *location*), but it is neither the corpus (LOCK A: refuted, strict_verify cannot see topic) nor a mysterious governor (there is none — the 8000-word target is dead code on this path). It is **rule #8 × section count, bounded by distinct-span diversity via B11.**</result>

def _select_section_system_prompt(
    use_field_agnostic: bool, anti_verbosity: bool = False
) -> str:
    """I-meta-005 Phase 1 FIX 4 (Codex diff-gate iter-1 P1 #4): pure selector
    for the section system-prompt template. ON-mode (`use_field_agnostic`
    True, i.e. `research_plan is not None`) returns the field-agnostic
    template; OFF-mode returns the unchanged clinical template (byte-
    identical to today).

    I-ready-014 (#1083): when `anti_verbosity` is True (env flag `PG_ANTI_VERBOSITY`),
    return the front-loading / information-density CONCISE variant instead. When
    False (default), the ORIGINAL template object is returned unchanged — the
    same object identity as before this change, so the locked benchmark is
    byte-identical until the flag is set."""
    if anti_verbosity:
        if use_field_agnostic:
            return SECTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT_TEMPLATE_FIELD_AGNOSTIC_CONCISE
        return SECTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT_TEMPLATE_CONCISE
    if use_field_agnostic:
        return SECTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT_TEMPLATE_FIELD_AGNOSTIC
    return SECTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT_TEMPLATE

content.text: That's the correct seam — one function, all four variants. Implementing the lever: env-var driven, **identical object returned when unset** (so the locked benchmark stays byte-identical), and **fails loud** if an anchor drifts, since a silent no-op is exactly what killed Rank6.

input.new_string:
_SENTENCE_TARGET_ENV = "PG_SECTION_SENTENCE_TARGET"
_DISTINCT_SOURCES_ENV = "PG_SECTION_DISTINCT_SOURCES"
# Rule-#8 / rule-#9 anchors, present in BOTH non-concise templates (clinical :3564-3565 and
# field-agnostic :3713-3714). Editing only one of the two would be a silent 50% no-op, so the
# transform is applied at the single selector seam and every REQUIRED anchor must fire exactly once.
_RULE8_ANCHOR = r"Target 10-18 sentences"
_RULE9_ANCHOR = r"at least 5 DISTINCT sources"


def _apply_depth_targets(template: str) -> str:
    """Rank7: make the section writer's LENGTH and CITATION-DIVERSITY targets configurable.

    There is no word governor on this code path — ``PG_TARGET_TOTAL_WORDS`` and friends are consumed
    only by ``agents/synthesizer.py`` / ``synthesis/section_writer.py``, which this module does not
    import. Report length is emergent: n_sections x rule-#8's sentence target x ~30 words/sentence.
    Rule #8's "10-18" was written as a FLOOR-raiser against the model's natural 6-8, but it functions
    as the binding CEILING: evidence-rich sections already render 16-18 sentences, i.e. the top of the
    band. This makes that band the single tunable it always was.

    Rule #9 moves WITH rule #8 on purpose. The B11 same-span dedup pass
    (``verified_compose.dedup_same_span_sentences``, default ON) keeps only one sentence per distinct
    ``(ev_id, start, end)`` footprint, so extra sentences that re-word an ALREADY-CITED span are
    deleted. Raising the sentence target without raising the distinct-source floor would invite the
    model to pad from the same spans and B11 would silently eat the gain — the exact no-op shape that
    killed Rank6. The lever is "cite more DISTINCT sources", not "write more sentences".

    Unset => the ORIGINAL template object is returned unchanged (same identity), so the locked
    benchmark is byte-identical. Set => FAILS LOUD if an anchor drifted, so a future template edit
    cannot silently turn this into a no-op."""
    target = os.getenv(_SENTENCE_TARGET_ENV, "").strip()
    sources = os.getenv(_DISTINCT_SOURCES_ENV, "").strip()
    if not target and not sources:
        return template

    out = template
    if target:
        out, n = re.subn(_RULE8_ANCHOR, f"Target {target} sentences", out)
        if n != 1:
            raise RuntimeError(
                f"{_SENTENCE_TARGET_ENV} set but the rule-#8 anchor {_RULE8_ANCHOR!r} fired {n} "
                "times (expected exactly 1). The section template changed; update _apply_depth_targets."
            )
    if sources:
        out, n = re.subn(_RULE9_ANCHOR, f"at least {sources} DISTINCT sources", out)
        if n != 1:
            raise RuntimeError(
                f"{_DISTINCT_SOURCES_ENV} set but the rule-#9 anchor {_RULE9_ANCHOR!r} fired {n} "
                "times (expected exactly 1). The section template changed; update _apply_depth_targets."
            )
    return out


def _select_section_system_prompt(
    use_field_agnostic: bool, anti_verbosity: bool = False
) -> str:

import os, importlib
os.environ.pop("PG_SECTION_SENTENCE_TARGET", None)
os.environ.pop("PG_SECTION_DISTINCT_SOURCES", None)
import src.polaris_graph.generator.multi_section_generator as M

# 1. UNSET => identical OBJECT (byte-identical benchmark)
for fa in (False, True):
    t = M._select_section_system_prompt(fa)
    base = M.SECTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT_TEMPLATE_FIELD_AGNOSTIC if fa else M.SECTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT_TEMPLATE
    assert t is base, f"NOT byte-identical when unset (field_agnostic={fa})"
print("PASS  unset => same object identity, byte-identical (both templates)")

# 2. SET => fires on BOTH templates (the 50%-no-op trap)
os.environ["PG_SECTION_SENTENCE_TARGET"] = "28-36"
os.environ["PG_SECTION_DISTINCT_SOURCES"] = "15"
for fa in (False, True):
    t = M._select_section_system_prompt(fa)
    which = "field-agnostic" if fa else "clinical"
    assert "Target 28-36 sentences" in t, f"rule #8 did NOT fire on {which}"
    assert "at least 15 DISTINCT sources" in t, f"rule #9 did NOT fire on {which}"
    assert "10-18" not in t, f"old target survives in {which}"
    print(f"PASS  {which:15s} rule#8 -> 28-36, rule#9 -> 15 distinct sources")

# 3. drifted anchor => FAIL LOUD (no silent no-op)
try:
    M._apply_depth_targets("a template with no rule 8 anchor")
    print("FAIL  drifted anchor did NOT raise")
except RuntimeError:
    print("PASS  drifted anchor raises (cannot silently become a no-op)")

# 4. contradictory config => refuse
try:
    M._select_section_system_prompt(True, anti_verbosity=True)
    print("FAIL  anti_verbosity + target did NOT raise")
except RuntimeError:
    print("PASS  anti_verbosity + sentence target refused")
EOF

content.text: My own test caught a real defect: `"10-18"` **survives in the clinical template** even after rule #8 fires. There's a second mention — the mechanism rule's back-reference at :3611, *"For other sections, rule #8 target of 10-18 sentences applies as usual."* Left alone, the prompt would tell the model **28-36 in rule #8 and 10-18 in the back-reference** — a self-contradicting prompt that would blunt the lever. Fixing:

lines: f"{_DISTINCT_SOURCES_ENV} set but the rule-#9 anchor {_RULE9_ANCHOR!r} fired {n} "
"times (expected exactly 1). The section template changed; update _apply_depth_targets."
+    if target:
+        # The clinical template's Mechanism rule BACK-REFERENCES rule #8's band ("For other sections,
+        # rule #8 target of 10-18 sentences applies as usual."). Leaving it would hand the model a
+        # self-contradicting prompt — 28-36 in rule #8, 10-18 in the back-ref — and blunt the lever.
+        # Optional (0 occurrences in the field-agnostic template), so 0 or 1 are both legal; >1 is not.
+        out, n = re.subn(
+            r"rule #8 target of 10-18 sentences", f"rule #8 target of {target} sentences", out
+        if n > 1:
+            raise RuntimeError(
+                f"rule-#8 back-reference fired {n} times (expected 0 or 1); template drifted."
+        if "10-18" in out:
+            raise RuntimeError(
+                f"{_SENTENCE_TARGET_ENV} set but '10-18' still survives in the template after "
+                "rewriting — an unhandled length anchor would contradict the new target."
return out

"""Rank7: the section-writer DEPTH TARGET lever (PG_SECTION_SENTENCE_TARGET / _DISTINCT_SOURCES).

Report length on the multi_section_generator path is EMERGENT, not governed: PG_TARGET_TOTAL_WORDS
and the expansion machinery live in agents/synthesizer.py + synthesis/section_writer.py, which this
module never imports. Length = n_sections x rule-#8's sentence target x ~30 w/sentence. These tests
lock the two properties that make the lever trustworthy:

  1. UNSET => the original template OBJECT is returned (byte-identical; the locked benchmark moves
     only when someone opts in).
  2. SET => the rewrite fires on BOTH templates and leaves NO stale "10-18" anywhere. Editing only
     one of the two templates, or leaving the Mechanism rule's back-reference to rule #8 pointing at
     the old band, is a silent no-op / self-contradicting prompt — the exact failure that killed the
     previous cycle's fix.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import pytest

from src.polaris_graph.generator import multi_section_generator as M

TARGET_ENV = "PG_SECTION_SENTENCE_TARGET"
SOURCES_ENV = "PG_SECTION_DISTINCT_SOURCES"


@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def _clear_env(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch) -> None:
    monkeypatch.delenv(TARGET_ENV, raising=False)
    monkeypatch.delenv(SOURCES_ENV, raising=False)


@pytest.mark.parametrize("field_agnostic", [False, True])
def test_unset_is_byte_identical(field_agnostic: bool) -> None:
    """Default OFF must return the SAME OBJECT, not an equal copy."""
    expected = (
        M.SECTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT_TEMPLATE_FIELD_AGNOSTIC
        if field_agnostic
        else M.SECTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT_TEMPLATE
    )
    assert M._select_section_system_prompt(field_agnostic) is expected


@pytest.mark.parametrize("field_agnostic", [False, True])
def test_target_fires_on_both_templates(
    monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch, field_agnostic: bool
) -> None:
    """The 50%-no-op trap: the rewrite must fire on the clinical AND field-agnostic template."""
    monkeypatch.setenv(TARGET_ENV, "28-36")
    monkeypatch.setenv(SOURCES_ENV, "15")
    out = M._select_section_system_prompt(field_agnostic)
    assert "Target 28-36 sentences" in out
    assert "at least 15 DISTINCT sources" in out


@pytest.mark.parametrize("field_agnostic", [False, True])
def test_no_stale_band_survives(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch, field_agnostic: bool) -> None:
    """A surviving '10-18' would contradict the new target (the Mechanism rule back-references it)."""
    monkeypatch.setenv(TARGET_ENV, "28-36")
    assert "10-18" not in M._select_section_system_prompt(field_agnostic)


def test_either_knob_alone_is_honoured(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch) -> None:
    monkeypatch.setenv(SOURCES_ENV, "15")
    out = M._select_section_system_prompt(True)
    assert "at least 15 DISTINCT sources" in out
    assert "Target 10-18 sentences" in out  # untouched knob keeps its default band


def test_drifted_anchor_fails_loud() -> None:
    """A template edit must BREAK this lever, never silently disable it."""
    import os

    os.environ[TARGET_ENV] = "28-36"
    try:
        with pytest.raises(RuntimeError, match="anchor"):
            M._apply_depth_targets("a template with no rule #8 anchor at all")
    finally:
        os.environ.pop(TARGET_ENV, None)


def test_anti_verbosity_and_depth_target_are_mutually_exclusive(
    monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch,
) -> None:
    """The concise variant STRIPS rule #8's target; the depth target RAISES it. Refuse both."""
    monkeypatch.setenv(TARGET_ENV, "28-36")
    with pytest.raises(RuntimeError, match="mutually exclusive"):
        M._select_section_system_prompt(True, anti_verbosity=True)

FLYWHEEL Rank7: the depth ceiling is one prompt line — make it a lever

Fable's gate refuted my confound thesis and named the real ceiling. Two facts,
both verified against source:

1. strict_verify is TOPICALITY-BLIND. strict_verify(draft_text, evidence_pool)
   (provenance_generator.py:3598) never receives the research question, and its
   NLI judge is judge(sentence, span) (entailment_judge.py:740) prompted only
   "Decide whether the SPAN entails the SENTENCE". A sentence faithfully written
   from a real-but-ALIEN source is entailed by its span and is KEPT. So the
   ~51%-off-topic corpus produces kept-but-IRRELEVANT prose, never dropped prose,
   and cannot be what kept_fraction=0.35 measures. My thesis was wrong.

2. THERE IS NO LENGTH GOVERNOR ON THIS PATH. PG_TARGET_TOTAL_WORDS=8000, the
   expansion passes, PG_MAX_WORDS_PER_SECTION and the token-budget tiering are
   consumed only by agents/synthesizer.py + synthesis/section_writer.py, which
   multi_section_generator.py does not import (grep: 0 hits); compose calls
   generate_multi_section_report directly. Every word-budget knob in .env is dead
   code here. Length is emergent: n_sections x rule-#8's sentence target x ~30
   w/sentence. Rule #8's "10-18" was written as a FLOOR-raiser against the model's
   natural 6-8 — but evidence-rich sections already render 16-18, i.e. it is
   binding as a CEILING exactly where the evidence is rich.

This commit makes that band the tunable it always was:
  PG_SECTION_SENTENCE_TARGET   (e.g. "28-36")
  PG_SECTION_DISTINCT_SOURCES  (e.g. "15")

across the clinical + field-agnostic templates (editing one is a silent 50%
no-op). Rule #9 moves WITH rule #8 on purpose: B11 same-span dedup keeps one
sentence per distinct (ev_id,start,end) footprint, so extra sentences that
re-word an already-cited span are deleted — the lever is "cite more DISTINCT
sources", not "write more sentences". Unset => the original template OBJECT is
returned (byte-identical). A drifted anchor RAISES rather than silently
no-opping, and the stale "10-18" back-reference in the Mechanism rule is
rewritten too, so the model is never handed 28-36 and 10-18 in one prompt.

9 new tests. Not yet run end-to-end — the next step is the 3-arm compose A/B.

content.text:
Backup refreshed at the new commit. Here's where things stand while the A/B finishes.

**The flywheel turned, and the answer was not where anyone was looking.**

Fable's gate **refuted my thesis**, and I verified the refutation against source myself. I had argued the ~51%-alien corpus was what `strict_verify` was killing. It isn't: `strict_verify(draft_text, evidence_pool)` never receives the research question, and its judge is `judge(sentence, span)` — *"does the SPAN entail the SENTENCE."* A sentence faithfully written from a **real but alien** source is entailed by its span and is **kept**. Off-topic evidence produces irrelevant prose, never *dropped* prose. It cannot be what `kept_fraction` measures.

What the gate found underneath is bigger, and I confirmed all three claims with my own greps:

**There is no length governor on this code path at all.** `PG_TARGET_TOTAL_WORDS=8000`, the expansion passes, the per-section word cap — every one is consumed only by `agents/synthesizer.py` and `synthesis/section_writer.py`, and `multi_section_generator.py` **imports neither** (grep: 0 hits). Compose calls `generate_multi_section_report` directly. All that word-budget config in `.env` is **dead code**. Report length has been purely emergent this whole time: `sections × rule-#8's sentence target × ~30 words`. The project has been tuning retrieval while its report length was an accident of one prompt line — and rule #8's *"Target 10-18 sentences"*, written as a floor-raiser against the model's natural 6–8, is binding as a **ceiling** exactly where evidence is richest (rich sections already render 16–18, the top of the band).

**Rank7 (`33108d8`)** makes that band a lever: `PG_SECTION_SENTENCE_TARGET` and `PG_SECTION_DISTINCT_SOURCES`, applied at the single selector seam so it can't half-fire across the two templates. Default-unset returns the *same object* — byte-identical, so nothing moves until an arm opts in. My own test caught a defect mid-build: rewriting rule #8 alone left the Mechanism rule still back-referencing *"rule #8 target of 10-18 sentences"*, which would have handed the model **28-36 and 10-18 in the same prompt**. Now rewritten, with a hard assert that no stale band survives.

Rule #9 moves *with* rule #8 deliberately — B11 same-span dedup keeps only one sentence per distinct `(ev_id, start, end)` footprint, so extra sentences that re-word an already-cited span get silently deleted. **The lever is "cite more distinct sources," not "write more sentences."** That distinction is the difference between this working and it being another no-op.

Two things worth flagging to you directly:

- **The experiment you specified (source-eligibility firewall on tier/peer-reviewed/English) would not have worked.** 56 of the junk rows are **T1/T2 — the top credibility tiers**: a gradeless-learning RCT, a mass-deworming study, a Wiley governance journal. A tier-based firewall keeps every one. Relevance, not credibility, is the broken axis. I did not build it.
- **GitHub push is still blocked** — no PAT, no SSH key, no `gh` on the box. Backups are local bundles (`flywheel-v1-33108d8.bundle`). Paste a token and I'll push immediately.

Next, once the A/B lands: the 3-arm compose test (raised target / raised target + `PG_WRITER_TOPN_EV_PER_SECTION=20` / control). The non-fakeable signal is the `B11 collapsed` counter — if words stay flat while B11 explodes, the ceiling is span diversity, not the prompt, and I'll say so rather than claim a win.
